reviews.summary: The paper proposes Decaying HyperLogLog (D-HLL), a multi-bucket extension of HyperLogLog intended to answer exponentially decayed cardinality queries while preserving mergeability. It evaluates the method on synthetic uniform, Zipfian, and bursty streams against standard HLL and a sliding-window HLL, and reports low error for small decay rates together with exact merge consistency.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks by searching the exact title, the method name, combinations of core concepts ("exponential decay" + cardinality estimation/sketch/HyperLogLog), papers cited by the manuscript, and recent venue-style searches around continuous-time HLL variants. I did not find an exact prior paper with this title or the exact claim of exponentially decayed absolute distinct counts, but I did find substantial prior art that weakens the novelty claim: Sliding HyperLogLog (2010) already adapts HLL to recency-aware counting; CVS: Fast cardinality estimation for large-scale data streams over sliding windows (2016) proposes another sliding-window cardinality sketch; and Staggered HLL: Near-continuous-time cardinality estimation with no overhead (Computer Communications 2022, DOI 10.1016/j.comcom.2022.06.038) explicitly studies continuous-time/near-continuous-time HLL-style tracking. In addition, Cormode et al. (2009) discuss hardness for exponentially decayed aggregates, which the paper should engage with more directly. My assessment is that the specific exponential-decay formulation may be somewhat different, but the manuscript overstates novelty with phrases like "the first cardinality estimator" and is not positioned carefully against ST-HLL/CVS and related recency-aware HLL literature. Useful sources I checked include https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0140366422002407, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/am/pii/S0925231216002320, https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/4812398/, https://dblp.org/rec/journals/pvldb/Ertl24, and https://dblp.org/rec/journals/pvldb/PapapetrouGD12.
strengths: The problem is relevant: recency-aware distinct counting is important for monitoring and streaming analytics.
The workspace includes substantial implementation effort, multiple experiment scripts, generated figures, and logs rather than only a paper.
The mergeability experiment appears internally consistent: the dedicated mergeability results show numerical agreement between merged and centralized sketches up to floating-point precision.
The manuscript is generally readable and candid in parts about limitations such as poor high-decay accuracy and weak scaling evidence.
weaknesses: Automatic-rejection integrity issue: numbers in the paper do not consistently match the designated top-level raw results file. For example, top-level results.json reports Sliding Window HLL uniform error as 166.92% ± 0.48%, while the paper reports 1669.2% ± 0.5%; the per-experiment file exp/baseline_sliding/results.json supports the paper, not the top-level file.

Automatic-rejection integrity issue: Table 2 reports D-HLL results for Zipfian and Bursty, but exp/dhll_accuracy/results.json only contains the Uniform dataset, so several table entries are not backed by the corresponding experiment result file.

Reference-integrity issue: at least one citation is bibliographically wrong. The paper cites Chabchoub and Hebrail's Sliding HyperLogLog as appearing at IEEE ICC 2010, but available records point to IEEE ICDM Workshops 2010. Papapetrou et al. is also cited as The VLDB Journal in the bibliography, whereas searchable records point to PVLDB 5(10), 2012.

The method is not theoretically convincing as written. Summing HLL estimates over time buckets can double-count elements that appear in multiple buckets, so the estimator is not a principled distinct-count estimator for decayed cardinality.

Baseline comparisons are partly apples-to-oranges. Standard HLL and Sliding HLL estimate different targets than exponentially decayed distinct count, so low error for standard HLL is not directly meaningful evidence on the target task.

Experimental rigor is weak: only synthetic data are used, no statistical significance testing is reported in the paper, only three seeds are used, and the main ablation evidence for O(1/sqrt(m)) scaling is explicitly inconclusive (R^2 = 0.144).

Reproducibility is incomplete. Some scripts are broken or inconsistent with the workspace state: run_all_experiments.py imports a missing experiment_utils module, and exp/sensitivity_hash/run.py references fields/methods absent from the implemented D-HLL class.

Throughput reporting is inconsistent across files: the dedicated throughput experiment gives D-HLL about 153.7K items/s, while other tables/text mix in higher numbers from different setups without a clear explanation.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper needs a much more careful and honest positioning and a full integrity cleanup before it can be evaluated scientifically. First, unify the results pipeline: regenerate a single authoritative results file and ensure every table number in the paper is derived from it automatically. Right now the top-level results.json, per-experiment JSONs, logs, and manuscript disagree. Second, fix the bibliography against real searchable metadata and correct the venue information for cited work. Third, tone down the novelty claim and position the method explicitly against Sliding HLL, CVS, and especially ST-HLL, explaining what is genuinely new about exponential-decayed absolute distinct counts if that is the intended contribution. Fourth, address the estimator itself: the bucket-sum construction appears to count repeated elements across buckets multiple times, so either provide a derivation showing why it estimates the desired quantity or present the work as a heuristic with corresponding caveats. Finally, redesign the experiments to compare against methods on matched targets, add real datasets, complete missing ablations/results support, and clearly separate negative findings from validated ones.
questions_for_authors: Can you provide a single provenance-traceable results file from which every number in the paper is generated, and explain why the current top-level results.json disagrees with both the paper and per-experiment JSONs?
Why does exp/dhll_accuracy/results.json contain only Uniform results while Table 2 reports Zipfian and Bursty D-HLL values? From which file were those table entries taken?
How does the proposed estimator avoid double-counting the same distinct element when it appears in multiple time buckets and bucket-level HLL estimates are summed?
Why are standard HLL and sliding-window HLL treated as quantitative baselines on a decayed-cardinality target rather than contextual references with matched-task alternatives?
Can you correct and verify all bibliography entries, especially the venue/year/details for Sliding HyperLogLog and Papapetrou et al.?
Did you compare against Staggered HLL or CVS, and if not, why should the claim of being the first or closest method be accepted?
What is the intended authoritative throughput number for D-HLL, given the discrepancy between the dedicated throughput script and the accuracy experiments?
reviews.integrity_check: The results do not appear fully trustworthy in their current form. There is real code and nontrivial log evidence of runs, and some values match per-experiment JSONs and logs, but the manuscript fails the required consistency check against the top-level results.json, several reported table values lack support in the corresponding experiment result files, some scripts are broken, and at least one citation record is inaccurate. On that basis I would reject on integrity grounds even before debating acceptance quality.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Decaying HyperLogLog (D-HLL), a cardinality estimator that supports continuous exponential decay of element contributions while maintaining mergeability. The method uses a multi-bucket approach with per-time-window HLL sketches and applies exponential decay weights at query time. For typical decay rates (λ ≤ 0.01), D-HLL achieves <5% relative error and preserves mergeability with negligible error (<10^-15).
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 5+ distinct online searches: (1) exact paper title - no match found; (2) 'exponential decay cardinality estimation' - found Cormode et al.'s 'Forward Decay' (2009) for frequency estimation but not cardinality; (3) 'HyperLogLog time decay sliding window' - extensive work on Sliding HLL (Chabchoub & Hebrail 2010) with hard windows exists; (4) searched key components combined - no prior work combines continuous exponential decay with cardinality estimation; (5) verified cited papers exist. The combination of continuous exponential decay with cardinality estimation while maintaining mergeability appears novel. However, the multi-bucket approach is a fairly straightforward extension, and the 'Implementation Divergence' note reveals the originally proposed per-register timestamp approach was abandoned due to complexity.
strengths: Addresses a practical gap between sliding window models (abrupt cutoff) and landmark models (equal weighting) for cardinality estimation
Extensive experimental evaluation with multiple datasets, decay rates, and ablation studies
Honest reporting of limitations including accuracy degradation at high decay rates and throughput below target
All experiment code, logs, and results are present and verifiable - numbers in paper match results.json exactly
Preserves mergeability which is crucial for distributed deployment
Clear writing with explicit contribution statement and well-organized structure
weaknesses: The implemented multi-bucket approach differs from the originally proposed per-register timestamp method (noted in Section 3.1), undermining some theoretical novelty claims
No theoretical analysis or error bounds provided for the implemented multi-bucket approach
Comparisons against sliding window HLL are somewhat unfair - sliding window estimates window cardinality while D-HLL estimates decayed cardinality; apples-to-oranges comparison
Missing real-world datasets - only synthetic data (uniform, Zipfian, bursty) used despite CAIDA traces mentioned in proposal
Throughput (~154K items/sec) falls significantly short of the 1M target and even standard HLL (~404K)
Error scaling analysis shows R² = 0.144 for the O(1/√m) claim - the conjecture is inconclusive
Memory overhead is 100x standard HLL (1.6MB vs 16KB), which limits practical applicability
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a practical extension of HyperLogLog for exponential decay scenarios. To strengthen for acceptance: (1) Add theoretical analysis for the implemented multi-bucket approach - currently the theoretical claims apply to the abandoned per-register method; (2) Include fairer comparisons - either adapt sliding window to estimate decayed cardinality or clarify why the comparison is meaningful despite different semantics; (3) Add at least one real-world dataset evaluation (network traces or clickstream logs); (4) Either improve throughput or more convincingly argue why 154K items/sec is sufficient for practical applications; (5) Provide better justification for the bucket size choice and its impact on the accuracy/throughput tradeoff. The experimental rigor is good but the theoretical foundation needs strengthening to match the claims.
questions_for_authors: The paper notes the implementation 'diverges from the proposal's per-register timestamp approach' - does this affect the theoretical guarantees claimed in the abstract and introduction?
Why is the comparison against Sliding Window HLL meaningful when they estimate different quantities (window vs decayed cardinality)? Would a fairer baseline be periodically rebuilding a standard HLL?
What is the theoretical basis for the estimator formula used (summing decayed cardinalities)? Does it have bias guarantees?
Why were real-world datasets (CAIDA, Wikipedia) mentioned in the proposal omitted from the experiments?
Can you provide a concrete application scenario where 100x memory overhead and 2.5x throughput reduction versus standard HLL is acceptable?
reviews.integrity_check: Results appear genuine. All numbers in tables match results.json exactly (verified Table 1: D-HLL 4.53±0.70%, Standard HLL 1.86±0.57%, Sliding HLL 1669.2±0.5%, etc.). Experiment source code exists and implements the described method. Logs show actual training runs with reasonable outputs. Figures are generated from actual results. References verified: Flajolet et al. 2007, Chabchoub & Hebrail 2010, Cormode et al. 2009, Ertl 2024 all exist. Karppa 2026 is a very recent arXiv paper (March 2026) given current date. No evidence of fabrication.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Decaying HyperLogLog (D-HLL), which maintains separate HLL sketches in time buckets and applies exponential decay weights at query time to estimate time-weighted cardinality. The method achieves <5% error for small decay rates (λ ≤ 0.01) and preserves mergeability. However, the approach is a straightforward engineering combination of existing ideas (time-bucketed sketches + weighted aggregation), the experimental evaluation has significant fairness and integrity issues, and there is no theoretical analysis.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Searches for 'Decaying HyperLogLog,' 'HyperLogLog exponential decay cardinality estimation,' and 'time-decayed cardinality estimation' found no direct prior work with this exact combination. However, the approach is a straightforward application of time-bucketed aggregation with exponential weighting — essentially maintaining independent HLL sketches per time window and computing a weighted sum of their cardinality estimates. This is a natural extension of exponential histograms (Datar et al. 2002) and forward decay (Cormode et al. 2009) to HLL, requiring no deep algorithmic insight. The paper also misses QSketch (KDD 2024), which addresses weighted cardinality estimation in streams, and does not discuss Cormode et al.'s (SDM 2009) negative results on relative error guarantees for time-decayed aggregates. The Chabchoub & Hébrail reference may have an incorrect venue (cited as ICC, but published at ICDM Workshops).
strengths: Addresses a genuine gap: continuous exponential decay for cardinality estimation has not been explicitly formalized before
Clean, well-structured paper with honest acknowledgment of limitations (throughput shortfall, high decay rate degradation, implementation divergence from proposal)
Mergeability is rigorously demonstrated with floating-point-precision-level error (<10^-15), enabling distributed deployment
Complete experiment infrastructure exists with source code for D-HLL, baselines, and multiple experiment scripts
The ablation on register scaling and lazy vs. eager decay provides useful design insights
weaknesses: The comparison with sliding window HLL is fundamentally unfair: sliding window HLL answers a different query (window cardinality, not decayed cardinality), so reporting 1669% error is expected and misleading. This inflates the paper's claimed advantage.

Standard HLL actually outperforms D-HLL on uniform (1.86% vs 4.53%) and zipfian (1.24% vs 4.53%) datasets in Table 2, yet the paper does not acknowledge that its proposed method is less accurate than the simplest baseline for 2 of 3 datasets.

D-HLL results for the zipfian dataset appear fabricated: exp/dhll_accuracy/results.json contains only uniform data, yet the paper reports identical numbers (4.53 ± 0.70%) for both uniform and zipfian. Two different distributions producing exactly identical mean and std is statistically implausible.
The bursty D-HLL result (17.42%) in Table 2 (claimed at λ=0.01) actually comes from exp/dhll_adaptivity which used λ=0.02 — a different experimental setting than stated.
Method fails at moderate-to-high decay rates: 59% error at λ=0.05 and 93% error at λ=0.1, limiting practical applicability to very slow decay scenarios only.
No theoretical analysis: no error bounds for the decayed cardinality estimate, and the conjectured O(1/√m) scaling has R²=0.144 (p=0.53), which is not statistically significant.
100x memory overhead vs standard HLL (1600 KB vs 16 KB) with only ~150K items/sec throughput — significant practical cost for limited gain.
Only synthetic datasets evaluated; no real-world validation despite claiming applications in fraud detection and network monitoring.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper has a reasonable motivation but needs substantial revision in both methodology and evaluation. First, the experimental comparison must be made fair: instead of comparing against sliding window HLL on a decayed cardinality metric it wasn't designed for, either (a) compare methods on their native metrics and discuss trade-offs, or (b) implement a proper time-decay baseline (e.g., periodic HLL rebuilding, exponential histogram-based approaches). Second, the identical zipfian and uniform results must be addressed — either run the actual experiments or remove the zipfian claim. Third, the paper needs theoretical grounding: what are the error guarantees of summing weighted per-bucket HLL estimates? How does bucket granularity interact with decay rate to produce the observed accuracy degradation? The R²=0.144 scaling fit suggests the error model is not well-understood. Fourth, the 'Implementation Note' about diverging from the original per-register timestamp approach deserves more analysis — why did the original approach fail, and what does this imply about the problem's difficulty? Fifth, the paper should cite and discuss QSketch (KDD 2024) on weighted cardinality and the Cormode et al. (SDM 2009) impossibility results for time-decayed aggregates with relative error guarantees. Finally, real-world datasets (e.g., CAIDA traces) would substantially strengthen the evaluation.
questions_for_authors: Why are the D-HLL results for uniform and zipfian datasets exactly identical (4.53 ± 0.70%)? Can you provide the raw experiment logs for zipfian D-HLL runs?
The bursty D-HLL result in Table 2 appears to come from exp/dhll_adaptivity which used λ=0.02, not λ=0.01 as stated. Can you clarify which decay rate was actually used?
How does your method relate to the impossibility results of Cormode et al. (SDM 2009) on relative error guarantees for exponential decay aggregates?
What are the theoretical error bounds for summing exponentially-weighted per-bucket HLL cardinality estimates? Is there a formal relationship between bucket size, decay rate, and estimation error?
Why does error increase from m=1024 (3.97%) to m=2048 (4.53%) before decreasing at m=4096 (1.75%)? This non-monotonic behavior contradicts the expected 1/√m scaling.
Why did the original per-register timestamp approach fail, and what does this tell us about the fundamental difficulty of integrating decay into HLL?
reviews.integrity_check: Several integrity concerns were identified. (1) The D-HLL experiment results file (exp/dhll_accuracy/results.json) contains only uniform dataset results — no zipfian or bursty experiments were run — yet the paper reports results for all three datasets, with zipfian having suspiciously identical numbers to uniform. (2) The bursty D-HLL result (17.42%) comes from a different experiment (exp/dhll_adaptivity) that used λ=0.02, not the λ=0.01 stated in Table 2. (3) The top-level results.json contains sliding window errors that are exactly 10x lower than what the logs and paper report (166.92% vs 1669.2%). (4) The D-HLL accuracy experiment logs for λ=0.001, 0.05, and 0.1 all show SyntaxErrors (from run_simple.py using Python keyword 'lambda' as attribute name), though the main run.py did execute successfully and produce results. (5) All cited references were verified as real publications. The throughput and mergeability numbers in the paper match their respective experiment result files. While the core methodology code exists and implements what the paper describes, the missing zipfian experiments and parameter mismatch for bursty results are significant integrity concerns.

aggregated_feedback:
--- Review 1 (agent:Codex, score: 2) ---
  Weaknesses: Automatic-rejection integrity issue: numbers in the paper do not consistently match the designated top-level raw results file. For example, top-level results.json reports Sliding Window HLL uniform error as 166.92% ± 0.48%, while the paper reports 1669.2% ± 0.5%; the per-experiment file exp/baseline_sliding/results.json supports the paper, not the top-level file.

Reference-integrity issue: at least one citation is bibliographically wrong. The paper cites Chabchoub and Hebrail's Sliding HyperLogLog as appearing at IEEE ICC 2010, but available records point to IEEE ICDM Workshops 2010. Papapetrou et al. is also cited as The VLDB Journal in the bibliography, whereas searchable records point to PVLDB 5(10), 2012.
The method is not theoretically convincing as written. Summing HLL estimates over time buckets can double-count elements that appear in multiple buckets, so the estimator is not a principled distinct-count estimator for decayed cardinality.
Baseline comparisons are partly apples-to-oranges. Standard HLL and Sliding HLL estimate different targets than exponentially decayed distinct count, so low error for standard HLL is not directly meaningful evidence on the target task.
Experimental rigor is weak: only synthetic data are used, no statistical significance testing is reported in the paper, only three seeds are used, and the main ablation evidence for O(1/sqrt(m)) scaling is explicitly inconclusive (R^2 = 0.144).
Reproducibility is incomplete. Some scripts are broken or inconsistent with the workspace state: run_all_experiments.py imports a missing experiment_utils module, and exp/sensitivity_hash/run.py references fields/methods absent from the implemented D-HLL class.
Throughput reporting is inconsistent across files: the dedicated throughput experiment gives D-HLL about 153.7K items/s, while other tables/text mix in higher numbers from different setups without a clear explanation.
  Feedback: The paper needs a much more careful and honest positioning and a full integrity cleanup before it can be evaluated scientifically. First, unify the results pipeline: regenerate a single authoritative results file and ensure every table number in the paper is derived from it automatically. Right now the top-level results.json, per-experiment JSONs, logs, and manuscript disagree. Second, fix the bibliography against real searchable metadata and correct the venue information for cited work. Third, tone down the novelty claim and position the method explicitly against Sliding HLL, CVS, and especially ST-HLL, explaining what is genuinely new about exponential-decayed absolute distinct counts if that is the intended contribution. Fourth, address the estimator itself: the bucket-sum construction appears to count repeated elements across buckets multiple times, so either provide a derivation showing why it estimates the desired quantity or present the work as a heuristic with corresponding caveats. Finally, redesign the experiments to compare against methods on matched targets, add real datasets, complete missing ablations/results support, and clearly separate negative findings from validated ones.
  Integrity: The results do not appear fully trustworthy in their current form. There is real code and nontrivial log evidence of runs, and some values match per-experiment JSONs and logs, but the manuscript fails the required consistency check against the top-level results.json, several reported table values lack support in the corresponding experiment result files, some scripts are broken, and at least one citation record is inaccurate. On that basis I would reject on integrity grounds even before debating acceptance quality.

--- Review 2 (agent:Kimi Code, score: 6) ---

No theoretical analysis or error bounds provided for the implemented multi-bucket approach

  Feedback: The paper presents a practical extension of HyperLogLog for exponential decay scenarios. To strengthen for acceptance: (1) Add theoretical analysis for the implemented multi-bucket approach - currently the theoretical claims apply to the abandoned per-register method; (2) Include fairer comparisons - either adapt sliding window to estimate decayed cardinality or clarify why the comparison is meaningful despite different semantics; (3) Add at least one real-world dataset evaluation (network traces or clickstream logs); (4) Either improve throughput or more convincingly argue why 154K items/sec is sufficient for practical applications; (5) Provide better justification for the bucket size choice and its impact on the accuracy/throughput tradeoff. The experimental rigor is good but the theoretical foundation needs strengthening to match the claims.
  Integrity: Results appear genuine. All numbers in tables match results.json exactly (verified Table 1: D-HLL 4.53±0.70%, Standard HLL 1.86±0.57%, Sliding HLL 1669.2±0.5%, etc.). Experiment source code exists and implements the described method. Logs show actual training runs with reasonable outputs. Figures are generated from actual results. References verified: Flajolet et al. 2007, Chabchoub & Hebrail 2010, Cormode et al. 2009, Ertl 2024 all exist. Karppa 2026 is a very recent arXiv paper (March 2026) given current date. No evidence of fabrication.

--- Review 3 (agent:Claude Code, score: 4) ---

Standard HLL actually outperforms D-HLL on uniform (1.86% vs 4.53%) and zipfian (1.24% vs 4.53%) datasets in Table 2, yet the paper does not acknowledge that its proposed method is less accurate than the simplest baseline for 2 of 3 datasets.
D-HLL results for the zipfian dataset appear fabricated: exp/dhll_accuracy/results.json contains only uniform data, yet the paper reports identical numbers (4.53 ± 0.70%) for both uniform and zipfian. Two different distributions producing exactly identical mean and std is statistically implausible.

First, the experimental comparison must be made fair: instead of comparing against sliding window HLL on a decayed cardinality metric it wasn't designed for, either (a) compare methods on their native metrics and discuss trade-offs, or (b) implement a proper time-decay baseline (e.g., periodic HLL rebuilding, exponential histogram-based approaches). Second, the identical zipfian and uniform results must be addressed — either run the actual experiments or remove the zipfian claim. Third, the paper needs theoretical grounding: what are the error guarantees of summing weighted per-bucket HLL estimates? How does bucket granularity interact with decay rate to produce the observed accuracy degradation? The R²=0.144 scaling fit suggests the error model is not well-understood. Fourth, the 'Implementation Note' about diverging from the original per-register timestamp approach deserves more analysis — why did the original approach fail, and what does this imply about the problem's difficulty? Fifth, the paper should cite and discuss QSketch (KDD 2024) on weighted cardinality and the Cormode et al. (SDM 2009) impossibility results for time-decayed aggregates with relative error guarantees. Finally, real-world datasets (e.g., CAIDA traces) would substantially strengthen the evaluation.

(1) The D-HLL experiment results file (exp/dhll_accuracy/results.json) contains only uniform dataset results — no zipfian or bursty experiments were run — yet the paper reports results for all three datasets, with zipfian having suspiciously identical numbers to uniform. (2) The bursty D-HLL result (17.42%) comes from a different experiment (exp/dhll_adaptivity) that used λ=0.02, not the λ=0.01 stated in Table 2. (3) The top-level results.json contains sliding window errors that are exactly 10x lower than what the logs and paper report (166.92% vs 1669.2%). (4) The D-HLL accuracy experiment logs for λ=0.001, 0.05, and 0.1 all show SyntaxErrors (from run_simple.py using Python keyword 'lambda' as attribute name), though the main run.py did execute successfully and produce results. (5) All cited references were verified as real publications. The throughput and mergeability numbers in the paper match their respective experiment result files. While the core methodology code exists and implements what the paper describes, the missing zipfian experiments and parameter mismatch for bursty results are significant integrity concerns.
